questions.text: I hold the view that the best outcomes come from shared decision-making.
I notice that even the memory of a strong emotion can produce noticeable physical sensations.
I find that even significant life changes don't produce the emotional turbulence others describe.
I believe in measuring what matters so I can manage it effectively.
I prefer relationships where people tell me directly when something is wrong.
I pick up on the way someone's breathing changes when they are uncomfortable.
I find that I make better decisions for others when I evaluate their circumstances without being influenced by how their situation makes me feel.
I find that my emotions are like a steady current — always flowing but never flooding.
I find that I'm most effective when my own life is in good order.
I feel that self-investment is the most reliable path to long-term fulfillment.
I rarely pick up on passive-aggressive comments in the moment.
I feel that building consensus creates stronger momentum in the long run.
I hold the view that the seriousness I bring to others' suffering is among the most important things I can cultivate in myself.
I find that taking care of others' needs before my own feels right.
I prefer to submit work quickly and iterate based on feedback rather than perfecting it first.
I'm comfortable leaving things unfinished, trusting I'll return to them when the time is right.
I frequently volunteer to be the spokesperson or point person.
I find that approaching others' pain with rational precision allows me to be more useful than approaching it with shared emotion.
